docs.title: PON.wiki
docs.text:
All things PON. GPON, XGS, EPON, etc. All in one place.

Welcome to PON.wiki! We house information about PON (and PON accessories).

Currently, we are creating an index of devices, both SFP and stand-alone for use as ONTs.

NOTE:

We need help with PON specifications, and explaining OMCI better! If you can help, please, submit a pull request!

docs.title: Login Credentials
docs.text:
SSH / SCP / UART Username Password ONTUSER SUGAR2A041

RX_LOS

This module outputs an signal when no received fibre light is detected. Some equipment may disable the interface when this signal is active.

Deprecated Encryption Algorithms

Some firmware versions use outdated algorithms for SSH and SCP, in this case it may be necessary to use the following parameters: 

-oKexAlgorithms=+diffie-hellman-group1-sha1 -oHostKeyAlgorithms=+ssh-dss

docs.title: Firmware
docs.text:
Backup FirmwareFlash Firmware

Run the commands:

fw_printenv committed_image 
fw_printenv image0_version
fw_printenv image1_version 
fw_printenv image0_is_valid 
fw_printenv image1_is_valid

If image0_is_valid or image1_is_valid are set to 0, assume there is no firmware installed for that image or that it may not be in a consistent state.

Image 0

If the commited image is set to 0 run the following commands to create a backup of the firmware:

cd /tmp
cat /proc/mtd > mtd.txt
cat /dev/mtd0 > 0_uboot.bin
cat /dev/mtd1 > 1_uboot_env.bin
cat /dev/mtd2 > 2_linux.bin
cat /dev/mtd3 > 3_rootfs.bin
cat /dev/mtd4 > 4_roofts_data.bin
cat /dev/mtd5 > 5_image1.bin

Once the backups are created, they can be copied from the ONU with the following commands:

scp ONTUSER@192.168.1.10:/tmp/mtd.txt .
scp ONTUSER@192.168.1.10:/tmp/0_uboot.bin .
scp ONTUSER@192.168.1.10:/tmp/1_uboot_env.bin .
scp ONTUSER@192.168.1.10:/tmp/2_linux.bin .
scp ONTUSER@192.168.1.10:/tmp/3_rootfs.bin .
scp ONTUSER@192.168.1.10:/tmp/4_roofts_data.bin .
scp ONTUSER@192.168.1.10:/tmp/5_image1.bin .

If image 1 is also valid and you would like to back it up, run the following commands:

fw_setenv committed_image 1
reboot

then follow the directions for image 1 below.

Image 1

If the commited image is set to 1 run the following commands to create a backup of the firmware:

cat /proc/mtd > mtd.txt
cat /dev/mtd0 > 0_uboot.bin
cat /dev/mtd1 > 1_uboot_env.bin
cat /dev/mtd2 > 2_image0.bin
cat /dev/mtd3 > 3_linux.bin
cat /dev/mtd4 > 4_rootfs.bin
cat /dev/mtd5 > 5_rootfs_data.bin

Once the backups are created, they can be copied from the ONU with the following commands:

scp ONTUSER@192.168.1.10:/tmp/mtd.txt .
scp ONTUSER@192.168.1.10:/tmp/0_uboot.bin .
scp ONTUSER@192.168.1.10:/tmp/1_uboot_env.bin .
scp ONTUSER@192.168.1.10:/tmp/2_image0.bin .
scp ONTUSER@192.168.1.10:/tmp/3_linux.bin .
scp ONTUSER@192.168.1.10:/tmp/4_rootfs.bin .
scp ONTUSER@192.168.1.10:/tmp/5_rootfs_data.bin .

If image 0 is also valid and you would like to back it up, run the following commands:

fw_setenv committed_image 0
reboot

then follow the directions for image 0 above.

To flash these firmwares on your G-010S-P stick, you must first upload them via SCP to the stick's /tmp folder like so:

scp firmware.img ONTUSER@192.168.1.10:/tmp/firmware.img

Then verify the md5sum of the file on the stick matches what you uploaded with

md5sum /tmp/firmware.img`

After that, determine if you are currently booted into image0, or image1 on the stick, either with:

fw_printenv committed_image

if that's 0 then you are on image0, and if that's 1 then you are on image1 Or, if you do:

cat /proc/mtd | grep image

if that says image0, you are on image1, and if that says image1, then you are on image0 Depending on which firmware image you are on, only one of the following steps will work:

Image 0 (Flashing Image 1)

mtd -e image1 write /tmp/firmware.img image1
fw_setenv image1_version PUTVERSIONFROMNAMEOFIMGHERE
fw_setenv image1_is_valid 1
fw_setenv target oem-generic
fw_setenv committed_image 1

Image 1 (Flashing Image 0)

mtd -e image0 write /tmp/firmware.img image0
fw_setenv image0_version PUTVERSIONFROMNAMEOFIMGHERE
fw_setenv image0_is_valid 1
fw_setenv target oem-generic
fw_setenv committed_image 0

Replace PUTVERSIONFROMNAMEOFIMGHERE in those commands with the name of the file you uploaded in the first step (without the .imgsuffix)

If you pick the wrong set of commands to run, the first one will fail, do not proceed further if that is the case !

After this, reboot the stick to boot the new firmware, and optionally do the same set of steps but for the other image

After that, determine if you are currently booted into image0, or image1 on the stick, either with:

if that's 0 then you are on image0, and if that's 1 then you are on image1 Or, if you do:

if that says image0, you are on image1, and if that says image1, then you are on image0 Depending on which firmware image you are on, only one of the following steps will work:

mtd -e image1 write /tmp/firmware.img image1

Image 1 (Flashing Image 0)

Replace PUTVERSIONFROMNAMEOFIMGHERE in those commands with the name of the file you uploaded in the first step (without the .imgsuffix)

docs.title: Login Credentials
docs.text:
Telnet / UART Username Password Privilege admin ADMIN123!@# 3 guest welcome 5 test default 4 Hardcoded Passwords

The guest and test user passwords are hardcoded in /usr/bin/cli.

docs.title: Images
docs.text: WAS-110

docs.title: Login Credentials
docs.text:
Web UITelnet / SSH / UART Username Password admin QsCg@7249#5281 user user1234

Changing Passwords

The password length isn't restricted from within the web UI; Passwords longer than 16 characters are sliced.

Password Storage

The default web UI password is stored in /ptconf/param_ct.xml. Modifications from the web UI are stored in /ptconf/usrconfig_conf and take precedence.

Username Password root QpZm@4246#5753 Weak Encryption Algorithms

The default dropbear host key /etc/dropbear/dropbear_rsa_host_key RSA/SHA1 algorithm may be disabled by newer SSH clients (disabled at run-time since OpenSSH 8.8, released in 2021). In such cases, it may be necessary to use the following parameters: 

-oHostKeyAlgorithms=+ssh-rsa -oPubkeyAcceptedKeyTypes=+ssh-rsa
